name: Poisoning, supply chain & RAG security
tagline: The most dangerous LLM attacks aren't in the live request — they're baked into what the model learned, the components you pulled off a hub, and the documents your RAG store retrieves. This module teaches the integrity/supply cluster of the OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) — LLM04 Data & Model Poisoning, LLM03 Supply Chain, LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses — at mechanism level, each landing on its defense: lineage and eval gates, provenance and signing, and permission-aware, tenant-isolated retrieval with citation integrity.
overview: Modules 1 and 2 taught the trust-boundary shift and the live-request attacks (prompt injection). This module covers the class of attack that is insidious precisely because it happens BEFORE the request: corrupt what the model learned, ship a tampered component, or plant a document in the retrieval store, and your runtime input/output filters never see it — the payload arrives pre-installed and wearing a trusted badge. We follow a timeline of trust across three OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risks that all attack the SUPPLY of data and components rather than the live prompt. LLM04 Data & Model Poisoning is corruption of what the model LEARNED — poisoned pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data that implants a bias, or a hidden backdoor trigger that lies dormant (a 'sleeper agent') until an attacker's phrase activates it; the 2025 title deliberately broadened the old 'Training Data Poisoning' to cover the model artefact itself, because a malicious model in a shared repository is a poisoning vector too. LLM03 Supply Chain is trusting third-party COMPONENTS you didn't build — a tampered base model or LoRA adapter from a hub, a malicious package, an unsigned model file with weak provenance, a compromised dataset; the defense is to treat models, adapters, plugins, and datasets like any other dependency (inventory them in an SBOM/AI-BOM, pin and verify from sources you trust, check file hashes and signatures). LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses is poisoning the RETRIEVAL layer at runtime — planting attacker text in the vector store so it surfaces as a 'trusted' citation (where indirect prompt injection meets RAG), plus cross-tenant embedding leakage when one customer's data bleeds into another's answers. MITRE ATLAS gives us the adversary's exact names for these moves — AI Supply Chain Compromise, Poison Training Data, Publish Poisoned Models/Datasets, RAG Poisoning, False RAG Entry Injection — and NIST AI 600-1 names the governance risk areas (Value Chain and Component Integration, Information Integrity, Data Privacy). Throughout, the honest posture holds: these are mitigable through defense-in-depth, not solved. Every offensive concept here is taught to build the defense, and every hands-on step targets your OWN workspace — the module's proof is answering, for one workspace, 'who can write to our retrieval store, and is it isolated?'
objectives: Explain why integrity/supply-chain attacks (LLM04, LLM03, LLM08) evade runtime input/output filtering — the malicious behaviour is installed before the request arrives — and place each on the timeline of trust (learned → component → retrieved).

Describe LLM04 Data & Model Poisoning at mechanism level: how poisoned pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data implants a bias or a dormant backdoor trigger (a 'sleeper agent'), why the 2025 title now covers the model artefact, and the pipeline-stage defenses — data provenance/lineage (ML-BOM, DVC), sandboxed vetting, adversarial-robustness and eval gates before promotion.

Apply LLM03 Supply Chain thinking: treat models, LoRA adapters, plugins/tools, and datasets as dependencies with a signed SBOM/AI-BOM inventory, pinned and verifiable sources, integrity checks (file hashes, signatures), and vetting of hubs and collaborative-development environments.

Explain LLM08 RAG poisoning and false-RAG-entry injection using the correct MITRE ATLAS techniques (AML.T0070 RAG Poisoning, AML.T0071 False RAG Entry Injection), and the defenses — controlling who can write to the store, validating and attributing retrieved chunks, and enforcing citation integrity so a planted document cannot masquerade as ground truth.

Diagnose cross-tenant retrieval leakage in a multi-tenant vector store and select the correct control (fine-grained, permission-aware embedding stores with strict logical partitioning and permission-aware retrieval) — tying it to the platform's workspace-scoped isolation.

Map the integrity/supply cluster onto MITRE ATLAS techniques and the relevant NIST AI 600-1 risk areas (Value Chain and Component Integration, Information Integrity, Data Privacy), and hold the honest posture that these risks are mitigated by defense-in-depth, not eliminated.

lessons.title: The supply of trust: poisoning what the model learned (LLM04)
lessons.objective: Explain why integrity/supply-chain attacks evade runtime filters, and describe LLM04 Data & Model Poisoning at mechanism level with its pipeline-stage defenses.
lessons.body:
## A different kind of attack surface

Everything you secured so far lives in the live request: a user sends input, the model responds, and you can filter both ends. This module is about attacks that never appear in the request at all. They are installed *earlier* — in what the model learned, in the components you assembled it from, or in the documents it will later retrieve. The payload is already inside the trust boundary before the first prompt arrives, so an input filter or an output guardrail simply never sees it. That is what makes this whole class insidious, and it is why you need a distinct set of controls aimed at the **supply** of data and components, not just the request.

Think of it as a **timeline of trust**, and we will walk it across three OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risks:

- **LLM04 Data & Model Poisoning** — corrupting what the model *learned*.
- **LLM03 Supply Chain** — trusting *components* you didn't build.
- **LLM08 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses** — poisoning what the model *retrieves*.

This lesson takes the first: LLM04.

## LLM04, precisely

OWASP's 2025 definition is exact: *"Data poisoning occurs when pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data is manipulated to introduce vulnerabilities, backdoors, or biases."* Read the three stages carefully, because the defense differs at each:

- **Pre-training** — the broad corpus the model learns general capability from.
- **Fine-tuning** — the smaller, task-specific dataset that adapts it to your job.
- **Embedding** — the step that turns text into the numerical vectors a retrieval system stores and searches.

Manipulate the data at any of these stages and you can bend the model's behaviour in a way that is baked in, not bolted on. Note the title: the 2025 revision widened the old *"Training Data Poisoning"* to *"Data and Model Poisoning."* OWASP is explicit that this reflects compromises extending beyond training data to the **model artefact itself** — a malicious model sitting in a shared repository is a poisoning vector, which is exactly where LLM04 hands off to LLM03 (next lesson).

## The mechanism that matters: backdoors and sleeper agents

The headline mechanism to understand is the **backdoor trigger**. An attacker with influence over training data teaches the model a hidden rule: *behave normally almost always, but when you see this specific, rare trigger, do this other thing.* OWASP names the effect directly — a backdoor that stays dormant until a trigger activates it produces **"sleeper agent"** behaviour. MITRE ATLAS gives the adversary technique its name: **Poison Training Data (AML.T0020)** — modifying the underlying data or its labels to *"embed vulnerabilities in AI models trained on the data that may not be easily detectable."*

Sit with why that last clause is the whole problem. A backdoored model **passes your normal tests**: on every input except the secret trigger it looks perfectly healthy, so accuracy metrics, spot-checks, and a demo all come back green. The malicious behaviour only surfaces on the attacker's chosen input, which your evaluation set will almost certainly never contain. This is the honest, uncomfortable core of LLM04: you generally cannot inspect a set of weights and see the backdoor, and you cannot rely on ordinary quality testing to reveal it. (We are teaching this at concept level only — the defensive point is that hidden triggers are hard to detect, not how to build one.)

Beyond backdoors, OWASP lists related patterns worth recognising: poisoned data introduced through techniques it names as *"Split-View Data Poisoning"* and *"Frontrunning Poisoning"* (both about getting malicious content into a dataset a victim will later pull), embedded malware via *"malicious pickling"* of a serialized model, and simple unverified training data that yields biased or erroneous output without any attacker at all.

## The defense: make the pipeline auditable, and gate promotion

Because you cannot reliably catch a backdoor by looking at the finished model, LLM04 defense is about **the pipeline that produced it** — provenance, controls, and gates — not heroic inspection of weights. OWASP's mitigations map cleanly onto pipeline stages:

- **Track data origins and transformations.** Know where every training and tuning example came from. OWASP names the tooling: *"OWASP CycloneDX or ML-BOM"* for a machine-learning bill of materials, and **Data Version Control (DVC)** to make tampering detectable by tracking changes to the dataset. ATLAS calls the control *Maintain AI Dataset Provenance (AML.M0025)*. If you cannot say where your data came from, you cannot reason about whether it was poisoned.
- **Vet sources and sandbox ingestion.** Vendor-vet data providers, validate outputs against trusted sources, and use *"sandboxing with anomaly detection filtering"* so unverified data cannot flow straight into training. Restrict which resources the pipeline can even reach (*"inadequate resource access restrictions"* is itself one of OWASP's listed causes).
- **Test adversarially, and monitor the training signal.** OWASP calls for *red team testing and adversarial-robustness evaluation* and for *monitoring training loss* for indicators of poisoning — an anomalous loss curve can betray manipulated data even when the finished model looks fine.
- **Gate promotion — the shift-left move.** The single most transferable control from traditional AppSec: do not let a model reach production on trust. Put an **evaluation gate in CI** — an adversarial and behavioural eval suite the candidate model must pass before promotion — so a suspect model is blocked automatically. This is the LLM-native version of the secure-SDLC quality gate: you are shifting the integrity check left, into the pipeline, where it belongs.

Hold the honest line throughout: none of these *proves* a model is clean. A determined, well-resourced backdoor can survive vetting. What provenance, sandboxing, adversarial eval, and a promotion gate buy you is **defense-in-depth** — each layer raises the cost and the odds of catching manipulation. That is the realistic goal for LLM04: not a guarantee of purity, but a pipeline you can audit and a gate that stops the obvious and much of the subtle.

## What this buys you

You can now explain, to an engineer who has only ever thought about the live request, why an attack on the training pipeline is a category they have been missing — and you can name the pipeline controls that answer it. Next we move one step along the timeline of trust: the components you didn't build.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why do LLM04 data-and-model-poisoning attacks generally evade the runtime input and output filtering you built for prompt injection?

options.text: The malicious behaviour is installed before the request — in the pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data — so it is already inside the trust boundary when the model runs, and a request-time filter never sees it
Runtime filters are simply configured incorrectly in most deployments, and fixing the configuration stops poisoning
Poisoning only affects the network layer, which input and output filters do not inspect
Output filters catch poisoning reliably, so it is not actually a filtering gap at all

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP defines LLM04 as manipulation of pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data — the corruption is baked into what the model learned, so it arrives pre-installed inside the trust boundary and a request-time filter has nothing anomalous to catch. Option b is wrong: this is a category the filters were never designed to cover, not a misconfiguration. Option c invents a network-layer framing that has nothing to do with training-data manipulation. Option d is the opposite of the truth — a backdoored model passes normal testing precisely because output looks healthy on every input except the secret trigger.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A vendor ships you a fine-tuned model. It passes your full accuracy suite and a week of spot-checks with no issues. Per LLM04, why is that NOT sufficient evidence it is free of a backdoor?

options.text: A backdoor is designed to stay dormant and behave normally on all inputs except a rare secret trigger, which your evaluation set almost certainly does not contain — so passing normal tests is exactly what a 'sleeper agent' model does
Accuracy suites are always statistically unreliable, so no model should ever be trusted regardless of testing
A backdoor would lower overall accuracy, so a high score already rules it out
Spot-checks over a week are too short; a full month of the same checks would have caught the trigger

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP describes backdoors that stay dormant until a trigger activates them ('sleeper agent' behaviour), and ATLAS notes poisoned training embeds vulnerabilities 'that may not be easily detectable' — the model looks healthy on everything except the attacker's chosen input, which your tests won't include. Option b overreaches into blanket distrust; the point is specific to hidden triggers, not that all testing is worthless. Option c is backwards — a good backdoor preserves normal accuracy precisely so it passes. Option d misdiagnoses the fix: running the same normal-input checks for longer still never exercises the secret trigger, so duration is not the gap — the input distribution is.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Given that you generally cannot inspect finished weights to find a backdoor, where does OWASP direct LLM04 defense effort?

options.text: To the pipeline that produced the model — data provenance/lineage (ML-BOM, DVC), sandboxed vetting with anomaly detection, adversarial-robustness evaluation, and an eval gate before promotion
To a single perfect scanner that reads a model file and returns a clean/poisoned verdict
To stronger runtime output filtering only, since that is where any bad behaviour eventually shows
To avoiding fine-tuning entirely, because only fine-tuning can introduce poisoning

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP's mitigations target the pipeline: track data origins with CycloneDX/ML-BOM and DVC, sandbox and anomaly-filter ingestion, run adversarial-robustness testing, and gate promotion — defense-in-depth around how the model was made. Option b is the silver-bullet fallacy the lesson rejects; no scanner reliably verdicts arbitrary weights. Option c ignores that a trigger-based backdoor produces normal output almost always, so output filtering alone won't reveal it. Option d is false on the definition — OWASP lists pre-training and embedding stages too, not just fine-tuning.

lessons.title: Components you didn't build: LLM03 Supply Chain
lessons.objective: Apply LLM03 supply-chain thinking to models, adapters, plugins, and datasets, using provenance, signing, and SBOM/AI-BOM inventory as the defense.
lessons.body:
## The second step on the timeline of trust

Modern LLM applications are assembled, not authored. A typical stack pulls a base model from a hub, layers a LoRA adapter someone else trained, wires in third-party plugins and tools, and feeds on datasets from mixed sources. Every one of those is a component **you didn't build** — and OWASP LLM03:2025 Supply Chain is the risk that one of them is compromised. Its definition is blunt: *"LLM supply chains are susceptible to various vulnerabilities, which can affect the integrity of training data, models, and deployment platforms."*

The mental model that makes this tractable is one word: **dependencies**. You already know how to reason about a compromised software dependency — a typo-squatted package, an abandoned library with a known CVE. LLM03 says: apply that exact discipline to models, adapters, plugins, and datasets, which are dependencies your industry got used to pulling with far less scrutiny than a pip or npm install.

## What OWASP actually lists

The 2025 examples are worth knowing by name, because they span more than 'a bad model file':

- **Vulnerable pre-trained models** containing hidden biases or **backdoors** (this is where LLM04's poisoning arrives *as a component* — a poisoned model pulled from a hub).
- **Vulnerable LoRA adapters** that compromise the integrity of an otherwise-fine base model. An adapter is small and easy to share, and it modifies behaviour — so a malicious one is a compact attack vector.
- **Weak model provenance** — no reliable guarantee of where a model came from or what was done to it.
- **Traditional third-party package vulnerabilities** — outdated or deprecated components, the classic AppSec supply-chain problem, still fully present.
- **Outdated or deprecated models** no longer maintained, **licensing risks** across mixed software and dataset licenses, **exploitation of collaborative development processes**, and **model tampering** through techniques OWASP names such as **ROME** (direct editing of a model's stored knowledge).

MITRE ATLAS gives the adversary's umbrella technique: **AI Supply Chain Compromise (AML.T0010)**, with sub-techniques that map straight onto the list — **Data (AML.T0010.002)**, **Model (AML.T0010.003)**, **AI Software (AML.T0010.001)**, **Hardware (AML.T0010.000)**, **Container Registry (AML.T0010.004)**, and **AI Agent Tool (AML.T0010.005)**. ATLAS also names the delivery moves: **Publish Poisoned Models (AML.T0058)** and **Publish Poisoned Datasets (AML.T0019)** — putting a poisoned artefact in a registry or repository so a victim pulls it. NIST AI 600-1 frames the governance risk as **Value Chain and Component Integration**: *"Non-transparent or untraceable integration of upstream third-party components ... improper supplier vetting across the AI lifecycle."*

## The central mistake: 'it's a popular hub model, so it's safe'

The assumption LLM03 is built to kill is that download count equals safety. Popularity is not provenance. A widely-used model can still have unknown origins, an unsigned file, a swapped-in tampered variant, or a malicious adapter recommended alongside it. Treating 'lots of people use it' as a security control is the supply-chain equivalent of installing any package that has a lot of stars — and the industry has been burned by exactly that in traditional software.

## The defense: inventory, pin, verify, sign

OWASP's mitigations are, gratifyingly, the mature supply-chain playbook applied to AI:

- **Keep a signed inventory (SBOM / AI-BOM).** OWASP calls for *"Software Bills of Materials (SBOM) with signed inventories"* — you cannot secure a dependency you haven't listed. ATLAS names the control **AI Bill of Materials (AML.M0023)**. Extend the SBOM to cover models, adapters, and datasets, not just code packages.
- **Use verifiable sources and check integrity.** OWASP: *"Use models from verifiable sources with integrity checks and file hashes."* Pin to a specific, vetted version; verify the file hash and any signature before you load it. ATLAS names the paired controls **Verify AI Artifacts (AML.M0014)** and **Code Signing (AML.M0013)** — sign what you produce and verify what you consume.
- **Vet suppliers and red-team third-party models.** Regularly vet data sources and suppliers and their security posture; conduct AI red teaming to evaluate third-party models before adopting them (a poisoned model, remember, passes normal tests — so evaluation must be adversarial).
- **Guard collaborative environments and patch.** OWASP calls for strict monitoring of collaborative-development environments (where 'exploitation of collaborative development processes' lands) and applying ordinary vulnerability controls and patching policies (it explicitly points at OWASP A06:2021) to the traditional package layer.
- **Manage licensing.** Maintain a licensing inventory and audit compliance — a legal-integrity failure in the supply chain, not a code one, but a real risk OWASP lists.

The honest posture holds here too. Signing and hashing prove a file wasn't altered *after* signing; they do not prove the signer's model is free of a backdoor. Provenance tells you *where* something came from, not that its origin was trustworthy. So LLM03 defense is, again, layered: inventory so you know your dependencies, verifiable pinned sources so you know they weren't swapped, signatures and hashes so you know they weren't tampered in transit, and adversarial evaluation so you have a chance at catching a component that was malicious from the start. No single control closes the supply chain; together they make it defensible.

## What this buys you

You can now look at any LLM stack and enumerate its non-code dependencies — base model, adapters, plugins, datasets — and say what provenance, signing, and inventory you have for each. That enumeration is exactly the LLM03 half of the module's hands-on step. Next, the third step on the timeline: poisoning what the model retrieves at runtime.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is the core mental model OWASP LLM03 asks you to apply to models, LoRA adapters, plugins, and datasets?

options.text: Treat them as dependencies — inventory, pin, and verify them with the same discipline you'd apply to a third-party software package
Treat them as trusted by default, because reputable hubs vet everything they host
Treat them as runtime inputs, to be filtered by the same guardrails you use on user prompts
Treat them as configuration values that never need integrity checks

knowledgeCheck.explanation: LLM03 reframes models, adapters, plugins, and datasets as dependencies — components you didn't build — so the mature software supply-chain discipline (inventory, pin, verify, sign) applies directly. Option b is the exact assumption the risk exists to kill: hubs do not guarantee safety, and popularity is not provenance. Option c confuses supply-chain risk with request-time filtering — a poisoned adapter is installed, not sent as a prompt. Option d ignores that OWASP explicitly calls for integrity checks, file hashes, and signed inventories on these components.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A team wants to add a LoRA adapter from a public hub to improve their base model. Which combination best reflects OWASP LLM03 mitigations before adopting it?

options.text: Add it to the signed SBOM/AI-BOM inventory, pin a specific vetted version from a verifiable source, verify its file hash/signature, and adversarially red-team the combined model before promotion
Adopt it immediately if it has a high download count, since popularity demonstrates it is safe
Rely solely on a runtime output filter to catch anything the adapter does wrong
Verify only the base model's provenance and assume the adapter inherits that trust automatically

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP's supply-chain controls stack: signed SBOM inventory, verifiable pinned sources with hash/signature verification, and AI red teaming of third-party components — and OWASP explicitly flags vulnerable LoRA adapters as able to compromise an otherwise-sound base model. Option b is the popularity-equals-safety fallacy. Option c leans on a single runtime control against a component that could carry a trigger-based backdoor invisible to normal output. Option d is wrong because the adapter is a separate dependency with its own provenance — trust does not transfer from the base model to whatever you bolt onto it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Your CI verifies a downloaded model's signature and file hash, and the checks pass. What does that actually prove, and what does it NOT prove?

options.text: It proves the file wasn't altered after it was signed (integrity in transit); it does NOT prove the signer's model is free of a backdoor or that its origin was trustworthy
It proves the model contains no backdoor, because signing validates model behaviour end to end
It proves nothing useful, so hash and signature verification should be skipped
It proves the model's license is compliant and its training data was clean

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Signing and hashing establish that an artefact wasn't tampered with after signing — real, valuable integrity-in-transit — but they say nothing about whether the signer's model was malicious to begin with; that's why OWASP pairs them with supplier vetting and adversarial evaluation. Option b overstates signing into a behavioural guarantee it cannot give. Option c is the cynical over-correction — verification is a necessary layer even though it is not sufficient. Option d confuses cryptographic integrity with licensing and data-lineage checks, which OWASP treats as separate controls.

lessons.title: Poisoning what the model retrieves: LLM08 RAG poisoning & false-RAG-entry injection
lessons.objective: Explain LLM08 RAG poisoning and false-RAG-entry injection with the correct MITRE ATLAS techniques, and the write-control, validation, and citation-integrity defenses.
lessons.body:
## The third step: the retrieval layer

The final step on the timeline of trust is the one you can most directly control on a running system. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) makes a model more accurate by letting it pull relevant documents from a vector store at query time and ground its answer in them. That grounding is a trust decision: the model treats retrieved text as *reference material* — closer to instructions than to untrusted user input. OWASP LLM08:2025 Vector & Embedding Weaknesses is the risk in that decision. Its definition: weaknesses in how vectors and embeddings are *"generated, stored, or retrieved"* can be exploited to *"inject harmful content, manipulate model outputs, or access sensitive information."*

This is the lesson where Module 2's indirect prompt injection meets RAG. If an attacker can get text into the store, and that text is later retrieved and treated as trusted grounding, the attacker has crossed the trust boundary through the *back door of the knowledge base* rather than the front door of the prompt.

## Two distinct mechanisms, named precisely by ATLAS

MITRE ATLAS separates two moves that are easy to blur, and the distinction drives the defense:

- **RAG Poisoning (AML.T0070).** The adversary *"inject[s] malicious content into data indexed by a RAG system to contaminate a future thread through RAG-based search results."* The essence: get a manipulated document into a location the RAG indexes, crafted so it reliably surfaces for a target query. When retrieved, it can carry false information, or an indirect prompt injection with malicious instructions. This is 'plant a poisoned page where it will be found and cited.'
- **False RAG Entry Injection (AML.T0071).** A sharper variant. The adversary introduces content *designed to be interpreted as a document by the LLM* inside a data source being ingested — so that a chunk of ordinary-looking content contains a *fabricated* 'retrieved result.' ATLAS notes two nasty properties: by hiding a false RAG document *inside* a regular entry, it can **bypass data-monitoring tools** and it **cannot be deleted directly** (there's no separate row to remove — it's embedded in a legitimate one). Attackers may manipulate the injected entry's metadata — title, author, creation date — to make the fabrication look authoritative.

Hold the framing the module requires: we are describing *how the trust boundary breaks* — a document the model treats as ground truth was actually placed by an attacker — not providing any injection string. The defensive value is understanding that retrieved content is attacker-influenceable, so it cannot be treated as inherently trustworthy.

## Why 'it came from our knowledge base, so it's true' is the trap

The failure mode is a category error: treating *retrieved* as *verified*. A RAG citation feels authoritative — it has a source, a title, maybe a date — so both the model and the human reading the answer tend to trust it. But a citation only tells you a document was *retrieved*; it says nothing about whether that document is *true* or who *put it there*. If your store will index anything and attribute nothing, a planted document inherits the credibility of the whole knowledge base. That is **citation integrity**: the property that a cited source actually is what it claims and came from where it claims.

## The defense: control the write path, validate, and attribute

OWASP's LLM08 mitigations, plus the ATLAS controls, form a coherent defense — and the biggest lever is the one most teams neglect: **who is allowed to write to the store.**

- **Control the write path (the highest-value control).** RAG poisoning and false-entry injection both *require getting content into the index*. So the primary defense is restricting who and what can add to it. OWASP calls for *robust data-validation pipelines for knowledge sources* and accepting data *only from verified providers*. In platform terms this is a permissions question: not everyone who can read the knowledge base should be able to write to it. Untrusted or externally-ingested content should be quarantined and validated before it can ever be retrieved as grounding.
- **Validate and classify what you ingest.** OWASP calls for *data review for combination and classification* — tag and classify knowledge-base content to control access and *"prevent data mismatch errors,"* so untrusted and trusted sources aren't silently blended into the same retrieval pool.
- **Enforce citation integrity and attribution.** Attach and preserve provenance on every chunk — where it came from, who added it, when — and surface that with any citation so a planted document can't masquerade as ground truth. Because ATLAS warns that a false entry may be embedded inside a legitimate one and *survive direct deletion*, treat the *source* as the unit of trust, not just the row, and re-validate on ingestion rather than assuming stored content is clean.
- **Monitor and log retrieval.** OWASP calls for *"detailed immutable logs of retrieval activities"* to detect suspicious behaviour — for example, one document that suddenly surfaces across unrelated queries is a poisoning signal.
- **Treat retrieved content as data, not trusted instructions.** Carry Module 2's discipline in: because retrieved chunks can contain indirect injections, the surrounding system should not let retrieved text silently override instructions or trigger tools without validation. ATLAS names the agent-side control *Restrict AI Agent Tool Invocation on Untrusted Data (AML.M0030)*.

The honest posture: you cannot guarantee every document in a large, growing store is benign, and a determined insider with write access defeats much of this. What write-path control, validation, attribution, and retrieval logging buy you is that poisoning becomes *hard to inject, easy to attribute, and observable* — defense-in-depth on the retrieval layer, not a promise that the store is pure.

## What this buys you

You can now explain, with the correct ATLAS technique names, how a knowledge base becomes an attack surface and why the write path is the control that matters most. The remaining LLM08 weakness — cross-tenant leakage, where retrieval crosses a boundary it never should — is the subject of the next lesson, and it is the platform's biggest live lever.

knowledgeCheck.stem: In MITRE ATLAS terms, what distinguishes False RAG Entry Injection (AML.T0071) from ordinary RAG Poisoning (AML.T0070)?

options.text: It hides content designed to be read as a separate 'retrieved document' inside a legitimate ingested entry — which lets it bypass data-monitoring tools and resist direct deletion, since there's no standalone row to remove
It only works against the training pipeline, not the retrieval store
It is a purely theoretical technique with no defined behaviour in ATLAS
It requires no content to enter the index at all, working entirely from the live user prompt

knowledgeCheck.explanation: ATLAS describes False RAG Entry Injection as embedding a fabricated 'document' inside a regular RAG entry so the LLM treats part of the retrieved content as a false result — and specifically notes this bypasses data-monitoring tools and prevents direct deletion. Option b confuses it with training-data poisoning (LLM04); this is a retrieval-layer technique. Option c is false — ATLAS defines it with concrete behaviour and an ID. Option d contradicts the mechanism: both RAG techniques require getting content into the ingested data source, which is exactly why controlling the write path is the key defense.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Both RAG poisoning and false-RAG-entry injection share one precondition. Which single control most directly attacks that precondition?

options.text: Controlling who and what can write to the knowledge base — validating and restricting ingestion so untrusted content can't enter the index in the first place
Increasing the model's context window so it can read more retrieved documents at once
Adding more user-prompt input filters, since that is where the malicious document arrives
Retrieving more documents per query so a single poisoned one is outvoted

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Both techniques require getting content into the index, so restricting and validating the write path removes the precondition — OWASP calls for data-validation pipelines and accepting data only from verified providers. Option b makes it worse if anything: a bigger window reads more of a poisoned document. Option c misplaces the entry point — the malicious content arrives through ingestion into the store, not the live prompt. Option d is a fragile heuristic; a document crafted to be highly relevant to a target query can dominate results regardless of how many are retrieved, and retrieving more untrusted content isn't a control.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A stakeholder argues, 'If an answer includes a citation from our knowledge base, it must be reliable.' What is the precise flaw, and what property fixes it?

options.text: A citation proves a document was retrieved, not that it is true or who placed it there; citation integrity — verifying and surfacing provenance (source, author, ingestion time) per chunk — is what closes that gap
There is no flaw; retrieval systems only ever index vetted ground truth by design
The flaw is that citations slow responses; caching them fixes the reliability concern
The flaw is fixed entirely by deleting any suspicious row, so no provenance tracking is needed

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Retrieved is not the same as verified: a planted document inherits the knowledge base's credibility unless provenance travels with it, which is exactly what citation integrity provides. Option b is the assumption LLM08 exists to break — stores index whatever gets written to them, and RAG poisoning targets that. Option c confuses a performance concern with a trust one. Option d fails against ATLAS's own warning that a false RAG entry can be embedded inside a legitimate row and resist direct deletion, so 'just delete the bad row' is not reliable — you validate on ingestion and attribute by source.

lessons.title: Cross-tenant retrieval leakage & citation integrity
lessons.objective: Diagnose cross-tenant retrieval leakage in a multi-tenant vector store and select permission-aware, logically partitioned controls, tied to workspace isolation.
lessons.body:
## The other half of LLM08: retrieval that crosses a boundary

RAG poisoning is content that shouldn't be *trusted*. This lesson is about content that shouldn't be *reachable*. In any system that serves multiple customers, teams, or users from shared infrastructure, the vector store holds many parties' data — and the cardinal rule is that a query on behalf of one party must only ever retrieve that party's data. When that boundary fails, you get **cross-tenant retrieval leakage**: one customer's confidential documents surfacing as grounding in another customer's answer. It is a confidentiality breach delivered through the retrieval layer.

OWASP LLM08 lists this directly. Under its risks: **Unauthorized Access & Data Leakage** — *"inadequate access controls enable retrieval of sensitive embeddings"* — and **Cross-Context Information Leaks and Federation Knowledge Conflict** — *"multi-tenant vector database environments risk context leakage between user groups."* NIST AI 600-1 frames the consequence under **Data Privacy**: *"impacts due to leakage and unauthorized use, disclosure, or de-anonymization of ... personally identifiable information or sensitive data."*

## Why it happens: similarity has no respect for ownership

Understand the mechanism, because it explains why this is a *specific* failure mode and not just 'a bug.' A vector store retrieves by **semantic similarity**: give it a query vector, it returns the nearest stored vectors. Similarity math does not know or care who owns a document. If Tenant A and Tenant B both have content about, say, quarterly pricing, and their embeddings sit in the same index with no enforced boundary, a similarity search for A's pricing question can return B's pricing document purely because it is *nearby in vector space*. Nothing malicious has to happen — the leak is the **default behaviour** of an unpartitioned store. That is the trap: teams assume retrieval is scoped because the application 'knows' which tenant is asking, but the scoping has to be enforced *at the store*, not merely intended by the app.

OWASP names a related third-party issue — **Embedding Inversion Attacks** — where an attacker who can reach embeddings can *"invert embeddings and recover significant amounts of source information,"* meaning the vectors themselves, not just the source documents, are sensitive and must be access-controlled.

## The defense: permission-aware, logically partitioned retrieval

OWASP's LLM08 mitigations for this are precise, and they are exactly the class of control the Automatos platform's workspace-scoped design implements:

- **Permission and access control at the store.** OWASP: implement *"fine-grained access controls and permission-aware vector and embedding stores"* with *strict logical partitioning*. The boundary must be enforced where retrieval happens — every query carries the caller's tenant/permission context, and the store physically or logically restricts results to what that caller may see. Intending to scope in the application is not enough; the partition must be real.
- **Classify and tag for combination control.** Tag and classify knowledge-base data so sources at different sensitivity or ownership levels are not silently combined into one retrieval pool — the same *data review for combination and classification* control that guards against poisoning also guards against cross-context mixing.
- **Protect the embeddings themselves.** Because embeddings can be inverted, control access to them at rest and in transit — the vectors are sensitive data, not opaque numbers.
- **Log retrieval immutably.** *Immutable logs of retrieval activities* let you detect and prove whether a boundary was crossed — essential for both catching leakage and demonstrating isolation.

## Tie-in: the platform's workspace as the tenant boundary

This is where the module's Apply-it-in-Automatos step gets concrete, and it is the biggest live lever of the three risks. On the Automatos platform the **workspace** is the isolation boundary: each workspace has its own members, its own Knowledge Bases, and — by design — workspace-scoped retrieval so one workspace's documents are not retrievable from another. That is precisely OWASP's 'strict logical partitioning.' And the write-path control from the previous lesson maps onto the platform's **roles**: a workspace's Admin and Member roles can *Upload documents* to the Knowledge Base, while a **Viewer cannot** — so 'who can write to retrieval' is answered by the role table, not left to chance. Auditing one workspace for *(a)* who holds write-capable roles and *(b)* whether its retrieval is genuinely workspace-scoped is the module's proof artifact.

The honest posture, one last time: logical partitioning and permission-aware retrieval are strong controls, but they are only as good as their enforcement — a misconfigured scope, an over-broad role grant, or an application that forgets to pass tenant context can reopen the boundary. So you verify isolation, you don't assume it; you log retrieval so a breach is detectable; and you keep write access least-privilege. Defense-in-depth, verified — not trust.

## What this buys you

You can now diagnose a cross-tenant leak as a store-level access-control failure (not a model bug), name the permission-aware, logically-partitioned control that fixes it, and connect it to the exact platform mechanism — workspace scoping plus role-gated writes — that you'll audit in the lab.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why can an unpartitioned multi-tenant vector store leak one tenant's documents into another tenant's answers even with no attacker involved?

options.text: Retrieval is by semantic similarity, which has no notion of ownership — so a query can return any nearby vector regardless of tenant unless a boundary is enforced at the store
It cannot — vector stores always isolate tenants automatically by default
Only a deliberate SQL injection can ever cause cross-tenant retrieval
Because embeddings are encrypted, similarity search randomly returns other tenants' data

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Similarity search returns the nearest vectors to a query, and nearness in vector space is unrelated to who owns a document — so without enforced partitioning, another tenant's semantically-similar content can surface as a default behaviour, not an exploit. Option b is the dangerous assumption the lesson corrects: isolation must be enforced, not assumed. Option c wrongly narrows the cause to injection; the leak here needs no attacker at all. Option d is nonsense mechanically — encryption at rest doesn't make similarity search return random tenants' data.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Which control does OWASP LLM08 prescribe as the fix for cross-tenant / cross-context retrieval leakage?

options.text: Fine-grained, permission-aware vector and embedding stores with strict logical partitioning, enforced at the store so every query is scoped to what the caller may see
A larger model, since more capable models won't reveal other tenants' data
A post-hoc output filter that scans final answers for other tenants' names
Trusting the application layer to remember which tenant is asking, with no enforcement at the store

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP prescribes permission-aware stores with strict logical partitioning — the boundary is enforced where retrieval happens, so results are scoped to the caller. Option b misattributes the fix to model capability; leakage is an access-control failure, not an intelligence one. Option c is a brittle last line that fails on any content without an obvious identifier and doesn't stop the sensitive data from being retrieved and used. Option d is exactly the anti-pattern the lesson warns against — intending to scope in the app is not the same as enforcing the partition at the store.

questions.stem: The module frames LLM04, LLM03, and LLM08 as a 'timeline of trust.' What do all three have in common that distinguishes them from live-request attacks like prompt injection?

options.text: They attack the supply of data and components — what the model learned, what it was built from, or what it retrieves — so the malicious element is present before the request and evades request-time filtering
They can all be fully eliminated by a single output filter applied at inference time
They only affect open-weights models and never affect commercial API models
They are all network-layer attacks unrelated to data or model integrity

questions.explanation: The cluster attacks the supply — learned data (LLM04), components (LLM03), retrieved content (LLM08) — so the harm is installed before the prompt and a request-time filter never sees it. Option b contradicts the whole module: a backdoored model or planted document behaves normally at the output, so no single filter eliminates these. Option c is false — a poisoned dataset, a compromised plugin, or a poisoned RAG store can affect any deployment, including ones fronted by a commercial API. Option d misclassifies them; these are integrity attacks on data and models, not network attacks.

questions.stem: OWASP renamed the 2025 risk from 'Training Data Poisoning' to 'LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning.' What does the broadened title capture?

options.text: That compromises extend beyond training data to the model artefact itself — e.g. a malicious model in a shared repository is a poisoning vector too
That only the model, and never the data, can now be poisoned
That poisoning is now considered impossible and the risk was downgraded
That the risk now covers physical hardware theft rather than data integrity

questions.explanation: OWASP broadened the scope to recognise that a compromised model artefact (for instance, a malicious model published to a repository) is a poisoning vector alongside manipulated data — which is also where LLM04 connects to LLM03 supply chain. Option b inverts it: the title adds the model without removing data — its definition still names pre-training, fine-tuning, and embedding data. Option c is the opposite of reality. Option d confuses this with a hardware or physical-security risk, which is not what the rename is about.

questions.stem: Which OWASP LLM04 mitigation is the clearest example of 'shifting the integrity check left' into the pipeline — the secure-SDLC discipline carried over from traditional AppSec?

options.text: An evaluation gate in CI that a candidate model must pass (adversarial and behavioural evals) before it is promoted to production
Adding more end-user prompt-filtering rules after the model is already deployed
Increasing the temperature setting so the model's outputs vary more
Publishing the model to more repositories to increase redundancy

questions.explanation: A promotion gate that blocks a model until it passes an eval suite is the LLM-native version of a secure-SDLC quality gate — the integrity check moves left, into CI, where a suspect model is stopped automatically. Option b is a runtime control applied after deployment, the opposite of shifting left, and it won't catch a trigger-based backdoor anyway. Option c is an unrelated generation parameter. Option d increases distribution, not integrity assurance, and if the model were poisoned it would just spread the problem.

questions.stem: Under MITRE ATLAS, which technique names the umbrella move of compromising the unique portions of the AI supply chain (with sub-techniques for Data, Model, AI Software, and more)?

options.text: AI Supply Chain Compromise (AML.T0010)
LLM Prompt Injection (AML.T0051)
RAG Poisoning (AML.T0070)
LLM Data Leakage (AML.T0057)

questions.explanation: ATLAS AML.T0010 'AI Supply Chain Compromise' is the umbrella technique, with sub-techniques including Data (.002), Model (.003), AI Software (.001), Hardware (.000), Container Registry (.004), and AI Agent Tool (.005). Option b is a live-request injection technique, not a supply-chain one. Option c is specifically the retrieval-layer poisoning technique (LLM08), not the supply-chain umbrella. Option d is about inducing a model to leak sensitive information, an exfiltration-style technique, unrelated to supply-chain component compromise.

questions.stem: A team pins their base model to a specific version from a verifiable source, records it (with its hash) in a signed AI-BOM, and verifies the signature in CI. Which OWASP LLM03 risk does this most directly address?

options.text: Weak model provenance and tampering — you now know exactly what artefact you're running and that it wasn't altered after signing
Excessive agency of the agent's tools at runtime
Unbounded consumption / cost of the model at inference time
The model producing confidently wrong answers (confabulation) to users

questions.explanation: Pinning to a verifiable source, recording it in a signed SBOM/AI-BOM with its hash, and verifying the signature directly attacks weak provenance and post-signing tampering — OWASP's listed supply-chain risks. Option b is a different OWASP risk (Excessive Agency) about tool permissions, not artefact integrity. Option c is Unbounded Consumption, a cost/DoS concern unrelated to provenance. Option d is Misinformation/confabulation, a model-behaviour issue that signing and inventory do nothing to fix.

questions.stem: An engineer says, 'We pull our model from the most-downloaded entry on a major hub, so supply chain isn't a concern for us.' Which correction is most aligned with LLM03?

options.text: Popularity is not provenance — a widely-used model can still have unknown origins, an unsigned or swapped file, or a malicious recommended adapter; you still need inventory, pinned verifiable sources, integrity checks, and adversarial evaluation
They're right — high download counts are an approved OWASP integrity control
Supply chain only matters for datasets, so a popular model genuinely removes the concern
The only fix needed is to add a runtime output filter; provenance is irrelevant for hub models

questions.explanation: LLM03's central lesson is that download count is not a security control — popularity says nothing about provenance, signing, or whether a tampered variant or malicious adapter is in play, so the full supply-chain discipline still applies. Option b invents an OWASP endorsement that does not exist. Option c is false — OWASP explicitly lists vulnerable pre-trained models and LoRA adapters, so models are squarely in scope. Option d misplaces the defense onto a runtime filter, which cannot substitute for provenance and can't reveal a trigger-based backdoor.

questions.stem: In a RAG system, why does the module say retrieved content deserves closer-to-instruction levels of scrutiny rather than being treated as inherently trustworthy?

options.text: Because an attacker who gets content into the index can have it retrieved and grounded — so a retrieved chunk can carry false information or an indirect prompt injection, crossing the trust boundary through the knowledge base
Because retrieved documents are always longer than user prompts and length equals risk
Because retrieval is slower than generation, which makes it inherently unsafe
Because vector databases cannot store text, only numbers, so their output is meaningless

questions.explanation: RAG grounds the model in retrieved text, so if an attacker can plant content in the index (RAG poisoning / false-entry injection), that text is treated as reference material and can carry false facts or injected instructions — indirect prompt injection meeting RAG. Option b invents a length-equals-risk rule that isn't the reason. Option c confuses a latency property with a trust one. Option d is mechanically wrong and irrelevant — embeddings index text that is retrieved and used.

questions.stem: Which MITRE ATLAS technique describes injecting malicious content into data indexed by a RAG system so it surfaces in future search results?

options.text: RAG Poisoning (AML.T0070)
Poison Training Data (AML.T0020)
AI Supply Chain Compromise (AML.T0010)
LLM Prompt Crafting (AML.T0065)

questions.explanation: ATLAS AML.T0070 'RAG Poisoning' is exactly this: injecting malicious content into data a RAG system indexes to contaminate a future thread via search results. Option b is Poison Training Data — corrupting the training set (LLM04), not the runtime retrieval index. Option c is the supply-chain umbrella, about compromised components, not planted retrieval documents. Option d is about iteratively crafting prompts to bypass defenses, a live-request technique, not store poisoning.

questions.stem: ATLAS notes that a false RAG entry can be embedded inside a legitimate ingested document. Why does this make 'just delete the malicious row' an unreliable defense, and what does the module recommend instead?

options.text: There is no standalone row to remove and it can bypass data-monitoring tools, so you treat the source as the unit of trust, validate on ingestion, and attribute provenance per chunk rather than assuming stored content is clean
Deletion is unreliable only because databases are slow; a faster database makes row-deletion a complete fix
It is fully reliable — ATLAS says embedded false entries are always trivially found and deleted
The recommendation is to stop using RAG entirely, as no defense is possible

questions.explanation: ATLAS says a false RAG entry hidden inside a legitimate one bypasses data-monitoring and resists direct deletion because there's no separate row to remove — so the module shifts to source-level trust, ingestion-time validation, and per-chunk provenance/attribution. Option b misattributes the problem to database speed rather than the embedding-inside-a-legitimate-entry mechanism. Option c contradicts ATLAS directly. Option d is the defeatist over-correction the module explicitly rejects — RAG is defensible with write-path control, validation, and citation integrity, just not perfectly.

questions.stem: A multi-tenant assistant occasionally cites Customer A's internal document when answering Customer B. What is the correct diagnosis and fix?

options.text: Cross-tenant retrieval leakage — an access-control failure at the vector store; fix it with permission-aware embedding stores and strict logical partitioning enforced at retrieval time
A model hallucination; fix it by lowering the temperature so it stops inventing Customer A's data
A prompt-injection attack by Customer B; fix it with a stronger input filter on B's prompts
Normal RAG behaviour that requires no fix, since both customers use the same product

questions.explanation: Real content from A surfacing for B is cross-tenant retrieval leakage — an unenforced boundary in a shared store — and OWASP's fix is permission-aware stores with strict logical partitioning enforced where retrieval happens. Option b misdiagnoses it as hallucination; the data is real and retrieved, not invented, so temperature is irrelevant. Option c wrongly blames the prompt path; the leak is a store access-control gap, not something in B's input. Option d normalises a confidentiality breach — sharing a product must never mean sharing a retrieval boundary.

questions.stem: OWASP LLM08 warns about 'embedding inversion attacks.' What practical control does this imply?

options.text: Access-control the embeddings themselves at rest and in transit — because an attacker who reaches the vectors may invert them to recover significant source information, so the embeddings are sensitive data too
Nothing — embeddings are irreversible numbers with no security implications
Store embeddings in plaintext everywhere, since inversion only affects encrypted vectors
Delete all embeddings after each query, since they cannot be reused safely

questions.explanation: OWASP notes attackers can invert embeddings to recover source information, so the vectors must be treated as sensitive and access-controlled at rest and in transit. Option b is exactly the false assumption the risk corrects — embeddings are not safely opaque. Option c inverts good practice and misstates the mechanism. Option d is impractical and unnecessary — the control is access control on the embeddings, not deleting the store's core data after every query.

questions.stem: Which NIST AI 600-1 GAI risk area most directly names the supply-chain concern of untraceable third-party components and poor supplier vetting across the AI lifecycle?

options.text: Value Chain and Component Integration
Confabulation
Environmental Impacts
CBRN Information or Capabilities

questions.explanation: NIST AI 600-1 defines 'Value Chain and Component Integration' as non-transparent or untraceable integration of upstream third-party components and improper supplier vetting across the AI lifecycle — the governance framing of LLM03. Option b (Confabulation) is about confidently false output. Option c is about compute/energy impacts. Option d is about eased access to chemical/biological/radiological/nuclear information — none of which name the supply-chain integrity concern.

questions.stem: Across LLM04, LLM03, and LLM08, the module insists on one honest posture. Which statement best captures it?

options.text: These risks are mitigated by defense-in-depth, not eliminated — provenance, signing, gates, and permission-aware isolation each raise cost and detection odds, but no single control guarantees a clean model, component, or store
With a signed model file, poisoning becomes literally impossible, so one control fully solves the cluster
Because perfect defense is unattainable, the controls aren't worth implementing
The only real defense is to never use third-party models, adapters, or RAG at all

questions.explanation: The module's binding posture is honest defense-in-depth: layered controls raise the cost and detection odds of poisoning and supply-chain compromise without ever proving purity — a determined, well-resourced attacker or insider can defeat any single layer. Option b is the silver-bullet fallacy; signing proves integrity-in-transit only, not a clean model. Option c is the cynical over-correction — imperfect controls still meaningfully reduce risk and are worth deploying. Option d is impractical absolutism; the goal is to make assembled systems defensible, not to abandon the components modern LLM apps are built from.

questions.stem: OWASP LLM04 and LLM03 both point to a machine-readable inventory of what went into a model. Which pairing correctly matches the artefact to its purpose?

options.text: An ML-BOM / CycloneDX record (LLM04) tracks data origins to make poisoning detectable; a signed SBOM/AI-BOM (LLM03) inventories model, adapter, and dataset dependencies so you can pin and verify them
Both are runtime output filters that scan generated text for poisoned phrases
Both are user-facing dashboards for end customers to rate answer quality
An SBOM is only for hardware, and an ML-BOM is only for network configuration

questions.explanation: OWASP LLM04 names CycloneDX/ML-BOM and DVC to track data origins and detect tampering, while LLM03 calls for signed SBOMs/AI-BOMs covering component dependencies you pin and verify — complementary inventories, one for data lineage, one for components. Option b confuses a bill-of-materials with a runtime filter. Option c misframes them as customer-facing rating tools. Option d invents scopes that contradict OWASP — an SBOM covers software/model/dataset components, and an ML-BOM covers ML data and artefacts, not just hardware or networking.

questions.stem: On the Automatos platform, the module says the workspace is the tenant-isolation boundary and roles gate the write path. Which statement is consistent with that design?

options.text: Retrieval is workspace-scoped so one workspace's documents aren't retrievable from another, and Admin/Member roles can upload documents to the Knowledge Base while a Viewer cannot
Every workspace shares one global vector store with no scoping, and any role can upload documents
Only the model provider, not workspace roles, decides who can write to a workspace's Knowledge Base
Workspaces isolate chat history but deliberately share all Knowledge Base documents across tenants

questions.explanation: The platform enforces workspace-scoped retrieval (strict logical partitioning, per OWASP LLM08) and gates the write path by role — Admin and Member can upload documents, Viewer cannot — which is how 'who can write to retrieval, and is it isolated?' is answered concretely. Option b describes the unpartitioned, un-gated anti-pattern the design avoids. Option c misassigns write control to the model provider rather than workspace roles. Option d contradicts isolation by sharing Knowledge Base documents across tenants, which is exactly the cross-tenant leakage the design prevents.

scenarios.title: Hardening a shared RAG store: a defense-decision drill
scenarios.tagline: The judgment call from the module's debate — where a multi-tenant RAG assistant, fed by third-party components and external documents, needs its integrity and isolation fixed without breaking it.
scenarios.context: You are the security engineer for a SaaS assistant. It serves many customers from shared infrastructure. Each customer's documents are ingested into a vector store and retrieved to ground answers. The team also pulls a base model and a community LoRA adapter from a public hub, and lets an automated connector ingest documents from customers' external sources. Two incidents just landed: a customer reported seeing a competitor's pricing detail in an answer, and a planted 'FAQ' document has been surfacing wrong, attacker-friendly guidance in support replies. You have authorization to change this system — it is your own product. Walk the hardening decisions. Every step has one best move; some alternatives are viable-but-weaker, and some are wrong.

steps.prompt: First incident: Customer X saw Customer Y's pricing in an answer. Where do you look first, and how do you frame the root cause?

choices.text: Treat it as cross-tenant retrieval leakage — an access-control failure at the vector store — and check whether retrieval is actually partitioned per tenant or merely intended to be by the application
choices.rationale: Real content from Y surfacing for X is the textbook LLM08 cross-context leak: an unenforced boundary in a shared store. The first move is to verify whether partitioning is enforced at retrieval (OWASP's 'strict logical partitioning') or just assumed in app code — that's where this class of bug lives.

choices.text: Lower the model's temperature and add a system-prompt line telling it not to reveal other customers' data
choices.rationale: This misdiagnoses a real, retrieved document as a model behaviour problem. The data was retrieved from the store, not invented — temperature and a polite instruction don't stop a similarity search from returning another tenant's nearby vectors. It leaves the confidentiality breach fully open.

choices.text: Add a final output filter that scans answers for other customers' company names and redacts them
choices.rationale: A post-hoc redaction filter is a legitimate defense-in-depth backstop, but it's a brittle last line: it fails on any leaked content without an obvious identifier, and the sensitive data was still retrieved and processed. Useful as a secondary layer, wrong as the primary fix — the boundary belongs at the store.

steps.prompt: You confirm the store isn't enforcing tenant boundaries at retrieval. What's the correct primary fix?

choices.text: Move to a permission-aware embedding store with strict logical partitioning, so every query carries the caller's tenant context and results are scoped at the store — then add immutable retrieval logging to detect and prove isolation
choices.rationale: This is exactly OWASP LLM08's prescribed control: fine-grained, permission-aware stores with strict logical partitioning enforced where retrieval happens, plus immutable logs to detect boundary crossings. It fixes the default-leak behaviour at its source and makes future isolation verifiable.

choices.text: Keep the shared index but prepend each document's text with its tenant name, trusting the model to only use matching ones
choices.rationale: This leaves the boundary unenforced and delegates confidentiality to the model's goodwill — the other tenant's vectors are still retrievable and can still surface. Enforcement must be at the store, not a hint the model may ignore or that an injection could override.

choices.text: Give each tenant a separate, isolated index or namespace so their vectors are never co-mingled
choices.rationale: Per-tenant isolation genuinely closes the leak and is a defensible architecture. It's marked viable rather than best only because it can be heavier operationally at scale than a single permission-aware store with logical partitioning; both enforce the boundary, and either beats a co-mingled index. Add retrieval logging regardless.

steps.prompt: Second incident: a planted 'FAQ' document keeps surfacing attacker-friendly guidance. It arrived through the automated connector that ingests customers' external sources. What's the highest-value control?

choices.text: Restrict and validate the write path — quarantine externally-ingested content, validate it before it can be retrieved as grounding, and accept from verified sources only, so untrusted documents can't silently enter the index
choices.rationale: RAG poisoning and false-entry injection both require getting content into the index, so the write path is the highest-value lever. OWASP calls for data-validation pipelines and verified providers; quarantining and validating auto-ingested external content directly removes the precondition for the attack.

choices.text: Delete the specific bad FAQ row and consider it resolved
choices.rationale: Deleting one row treats the symptom, not the open ingestion path that let it in — more will follow. And ATLAS warns a false RAG entry can be embedded inside a legitimate document and resist direct deletion, so 'delete the row' isn't even reliable for this class.

choices.text: Attach provenance to every chunk and surface it with citations, so a planted document is visibly un-attributed
choices.rationale: Citation integrity is a real and important control — it helps humans and downstream logic spot an untrusted source — but on its own it detects and attributes rather than prevents; the poisoned document still entered and was retrieved. Best paired with, not substituted for, write-path validation.

steps.prompt: During the review you realise the base model and its community LoRA adapter were pulled from a public hub with no verification. How do you fold the supply-chain side into the fix?

choices.text: Inventory the model, adapter, and datasets in a signed AI-BOM; pin them to specific vetted versions from verifiable sources; verify hashes/signatures in CI; and adversarially evaluate the combined model before promotion
choices.rationale: This is OWASP LLM03's full playbook applied to the actual dependencies: signed SBOM/AI-BOM inventory, pinned verifiable sources, integrity verification, and AI red-teaming of third-party components (a poisoned model passes normal tests, so evaluation must be adversarial). It closes the 'popularity is not provenance' gap the team fell into.

choices.text: Keep using them unchanged — they're popular on the hub, so their download count is sufficient assurance
choices.rationale: This is the exact fallacy LLM03 exists to kill: popularity is not provenance. A widely-downloaded model or adapter can still have unknown origins, an unsigned file, or a malicious variant — and OWASP specifically flags vulnerable LoRA adapters as able to compromise a sound base model.

choices.text: Pin and hash-verify the base model now, and add the adapter and datasets to the inventory in a later hardening pass
choices.rationale: Pinning and verifying the base model is real progress and better than nothing, but leaving the adapter and datasets unverified keeps live supply-chain exposure open — the adapter modifies behaviour and is a compact attack vector. Acceptable as a sequenced first step only if the remaining components are tracked and closed promptly, not indefinitely deferred.

steps.prompt: Leadership asks you to sign off that the assistant is now 'secure against poisoning and supply-chain attacks.' How do you respond?

choices.text: Report the risk as substantially reduced through defense-in-depth — enforced isolation, validated write path, citation integrity, signed/pinned components, and an eval gate — while stating plainly that these mitigate rather than eliminate: a determined insider or well-resourced backdoor can still defeat a single layer, so we verify and monitor rather than declare it solved
choices.rationale: This is the module's binding honest posture. The layered controls genuinely and substantially reduce risk, but none proves purity — signing is integrity-in-transit, a backdoor can pass evaluation, an insider with write access defeats much of it. The correct sign-off is 'materially hardened and monitored,' never 'solved.'

choices.text: Sign off that it is now fully secure and poisoning is impossible, since every recommended control is in place
choices.rationale: This is the silver-bullet fallacy the module explicitly rejects. No combination of these controls guarantees a clean model, component, or store; claiming 'impossible' misleads leadership and creates false assurance that will not survive a determined attacker or insider.

choices.text: Refuse to sign anything, arguing that since perfect security is impossible the controls give no meaningful assurance
choices.rationale: This is the cynical over-correction. Defense-in-depth meaningfully lowers cost and raises detection odds even though it isn't perfect — that reduction is exactly what a security sign-off should report. Both 'it's solved' and 'it's hopeless' are wrong; the honest answer is 'materially reduced and monitored.'

labs.title: Who can write to retrieval, and is it isolated? — audit one workspace
labs.goal: Produce the module's proof artifact: a short written answer, for ONE Automatos workspace, to two questions that decide your LLM08 exposure — (1) who can write to the RAG / knowledge stores, and (2) is retrieval genuinely tenant-isolated to this workspace? Plus a starter LLM03 dependency inventory for the agent(s) that read from it. Everything here is a defensive audit of a system you own — never probe a workspace, tenant, or system you don't own or have written authorization for.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

You'll run a hands-on integrity audit of your own workspace's retrieval layer, mapping what you learned in Lessons 3 and 4 onto the real controls the platform exposes. This is inspection and configuration review — not attacking anything. You are answering the module's core question for one workspace so you can see your own poisoning-and-leakage exposure and close the obvious gaps.

### Before you start

- **Sign in and confirm your workspace.** A personal workspace is created for you on first login and you're its admin, so every step here is achievable solo. Note the workspace you're auditing by name.
- **Frame the authorization.** You are auditing a system you own. If you ever extend this to a shared or employer workspace, do it only with explicit authorization and the admin's knowledge. The whole point of this module is defensive.

### Part A — Who can WRITE to retrieval? (the LLM08 write-path control)

1. **Open the Knowledge Bases page.** This is where documents, databases, and code repositories your agents can search and reference live — the Documents tab (Library, Upload, Search, RAG) is your retrieval store's front door.
2. **List who can add to it.** Open Team Management and read the roles. On the platform, **Admin** and **Member** can upload documents; a **Viewer** cannot. Write down every member and their role, and mark who is therefore write-capable to the Knowledge Base.
3. **Apply least privilege to the write path.** For each write-capable member, ask: do they actually need to add documents, or should they be a Viewer? Anyone who only needs to read answers does not need write access. Note any role you'd tighten — over-broad write access is your biggest RAG-poisoning exposure.
4. **Check external / automated ingestion.** If any documents arrive through cloud sync or an automated connector rather than a human upload, treat that as an untrusted write path: note what it ingests and whether that content is validated before it becomes retrievable grounding. Unvalidated auto-ingestion is exactly the RAG-poisoning vector from Lesson 3.

### Part B — Is retrieval ISOLATED to this workspace? (the LLM08 partitioning control)

5. **Confirm the boundary.** The workspace is the platform's tenant-isolation boundary — its Knowledge Bases and retrieval are workspace-scoped by design. Verify your understanding: documents in this workspace should not be retrievable from another workspace, and vice versa.
6. **Test it with a retrieval probe (read-only).** Use the Documents **Search** tab (or ask an agent in Chat a question whose answer would only exist in this workspace's documents). Confirm the results come only from this workspace's content. If you have access to a second workspace you own, confirm the same query there does NOT return this workspace's documents. This is your hands-on isolation check — observing scoping, not breaking it.
7. **Note citation/provenance.** When an agent answers from the Knowledge Base, check whether it surfaces where the information came from. Citation integrity is what stops a planted document masquerading as ground truth — note whether you can tell a retrieved answer's source, and flag it if you can't.

### Part C — Starter LLM03 dependency inventory

8. **List the non-code dependencies of the agent(s) that read this store.** In a note, write down: the model(s) the agent uses, any adapters, and any connected tools/plugins (for example, Composio components) it can call. This is the beginning of an AI-BOM. For each, note whether you know its source and version — you're not signing anything today, you're establishing what you'd need to pin and verify.

### Produce the artifact

9. **Write the two-question answer.** In a document you keep, record for this workspace: **(1) Who can write to retrieval** — the list of write-capable members plus any automated ingestion path, and which you'd tighten — and **(2) Is retrieval isolated** — your evidence that retrieval is workspace-scoped, plus whether citations expose provenance. Append the Part C dependency list. This is the module's proof.

### Why it matters

LLM08 is the biggest *live* lever of the three risks in this module: unlike a poisoned pre-training run you can't inspect, the write path and the isolation boundary are things you can check and fix on a running system today. Answering 'who can write, and is it isolated?' for one workspace is the smallest complete version of the control that stops a planted document from being cited as truth or leaking across tenants. The LLM03 inventory seeds the supply-chain discipline you'd extend next.

### A note on cost

The retrieval probe in step 6 runs a chat/search that uses an AI model, and models cost money per use. The realistic model for an operator is bring-your-own-key — supply your provider key in Settings and pay the provider directly. This audit is short and cheap, but it's worth knowing; the track's cost module covers this properly.
checklist: Signed in and named the single workspace you are auditing (a system you own)
Opened Knowledge Bases and identified the retrieval store (Documents tab: Library / Upload / Search / RAG)
Listed every workspace member with their role and marked who is write-capable (Admin/Member can upload; Viewer cannot)
Flagged at least one role you would tighten toward least privilege on the write path, OR justified that current write access is already minimal
Identified any external/automated ingestion path (cloud sync/connector) and noted whether its content is validated before becoming retrievable
Confirmed the workspace is the isolation boundary and ran a read-only retrieval probe (Search tab or Chat) showing results come only from this workspace's content
Noted whether agent answers surface provenance/citations (citation integrity) — flagged if source is not visible
Started an LLM03 dependency inventory (AI-BOM): the agent's model(s), any adapters, and connected tools/plugins, with source/version where known
ARTIFACT SAVED: a document answering (1) who can write to retrieval and what you'd tighten, and (2) evidence retrieval is workspace-isolated, plus the dependency list

resources.title: OWASP LLM03:2025 Supply Chain
resources.annotation: The primary source for Lesson 2. Read the definition and the numbered common examples — vulnerable pre-trained models with hidden backdoors, vulnerable LoRA adapters, weak model provenance, licensing risk, model tampering (e.g. ROME) — and the mitigations: signed SBOMs, models from verifiable sources with integrity checks and file hashes, AI red teaming of third-party models, and vetting of collaborative-development environments. This is where 'treat models, adapters, plugins, and datasets as dependencies' comes from.

resources.title: OWASP LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning
resources.annotation: The primary source for Lesson 1. Note the exact definition (pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data manipulated to introduce vulnerabilities, backdoors, or biases), why the 2025 title broadened to include the model artefact, the 'sleeper agent' backdoor behaviour, and the pipeline defenses: track data origins with CycloneDX/ML-BOM and Data Version Control, sandbox and anomaly-filter ingestion, adversarial-robustness testing, and monitoring training loss. The eval-gate-before-promotion framing is the shift-left / secure-SDLC bridge.

resources.title: OWASP LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses
resources.annotation: The primary source for Lessons 3 and 4. Covers RAG-specific risk: unauthorized access and data leakage from inadequate access controls, cross-context leaks in multi-tenant vector databases, embedding-inversion attacks, and data-poisoning of retrieval. The mitigations are the module's spine for RAG defense — permission-aware vector/embedding stores with strict logical partitioning, data-validation pipelines accepting only verified providers, data review for combination and classification, and immutable retrieval logs.

resources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) — the list
resources.annotation: The parent list from the OWASP Gen AI Security Project. Use it to place this module's three risks (LLM03, LLM04, LLM08) in the full 2025 set and to keep the current identifiers and titles straight — the whole AI-Security track's threat spine maps to this page. Skim it once so the 'timeline of trust' cluster sits in context alongside LLM01 Prompt Injection and the rest.

resources.title: MITRE ATLAS — Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems
resources.annotation: The adversary's vocabulary for this module, modelled after MITRE ATT&CK. The techniques used here are all live in the current matrix: AI Supply Chain Compromise (AML.T0010) with its Data/Model/AI Software/Hardware/Container Registry/AI Agent Tool sub-techniques, Poison Training Data (AML.T0020), Publish Poisoned Models (AML.T0058) and Datasets (AML.T0019), and the two retrieval techniques — RAG Poisoning (AML.T0070) and False RAG Entry Injection (AML.T0071). Browse the matrix to see how these chain into a real attack path.

resources.title: NIST AI 600-1 — Generative AI Profile (AI RMF companion)
resources.annotation: The governance overlay (July 2024), a cross-sectoral profile companion to the voluntary NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, built on four core functions: GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE). For this module, read the risk areas that name the integrity/supply cluster: 'Value Chain and Component Integration' (untraceable third-party components and poor supplier vetting = LLM03), 'Information Integrity' (mis/disinformation = the misinformation edge of RAG poisoning), and 'Data Privacy' (leakage and unauthorized disclosure = cross-tenant retrieval leakage). It frames the module's technical controls as organizational risk management: MAP identifies these risks, MEASURE evaluates them (eval gates, red-teaming), MANAGE applies the mitigations, and GOVERN sets the policy that requires provenance, signing, and isolation.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Knowledge Bases (platform docs)
resources.annotation: The platform surface you'll audit in the lab. The Knowledge Bases page manages the documents, databases, and code repositories agents can search — the Documents tab (Library, Upload, Search, RAG) is your workspace's retrieval store. Read it to find where to check what's in the store, run a read-only Search probe for the isolation test, and see the RAG configuration. The workspace is the tenant-isolation boundary that makes retrieval workspace-scoped.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Roles & Permissions (platform docs)
resources.annotation: The write-path control for the lab's Part A. Three workspace roles — Admin, Member, Viewer. The 'Upload documents' permission is Admin/Member only; a Viewer cannot write to the Knowledge Base. This role table is exactly how you answer 'who can write to retrieval?' concretely and apply least privilege to the RAG write path — the highest-value defense against RAG poisoning.
